draft.introduction: Galen’s On the Natural Faculties defends a teleological view of the body, arguing that each organ possesses natural powers of attraction, retention, and expulsion. It dominated medical theory for over a millennium until the Scientific Revolution.

translation.text: Since feeling and voluntary motion are peculiar to animals, while growth and nutrition are common to plants as well, we may justly call the former powers of the soul psychic, and the latter natural. Both the Stoics and Aristotle agree on this point. Growth, nutrition, and generation constitute the natural faculties.
translation.original_text: Ἐπειδὴ τὸ μὲν αἰσθάνεσθαι καὶ κινεῖσθαι καθ’ ὁρμὴν ἴδιον τῶν ζῴων ἐστί

translator_notes.note: Galen’s synthesis of Hippocratic medicine, Aristotelian teleology, and Platonic psychology created the medical paradigm that endured until Harvey’s discovery of blood circulation (1628). His insistence on empirical anatomy combined with philosophical framework made him the most influential physician in history.
